next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

By the third voicemail Nate understood that Sunday dinner was not a suggestion. Renata Altieri's messages had escalated in under two hours from checking in to prosecution. The flip phone had spent the afternoon vibrating across the kitchen counter like an angry insect, bumping the sugar bowl a quarter inch at a time. Nate had missed the last two Sundays because nobody had informed him that being Dominic meant reporting to a small colonial somewhere across Bridgeport to eat until you were forgiven for whatever you had done that week. Renata had apparently decided the grace period was over.

The first message asked where he was. The second wanted to know if Kassos Development had started charging overtime for neglecting your mother. The third informed him that if she had to keep the pork in the oven one more hour it would turn to rope and he could choke on it. Nate listened to all three while standing in Dominic's kitchen with his keys in one hand and no idea what version of son he was supposed to bring.

He drove with the air conditioning on high and still arrived with his shirt sticking between his shoulder blades. The August heat sat low over the city and made everything feel touched already, the steering wheel tacky, the seatbelt damp where it crossed his neck. Dominic's Honda Civic rattled over patched streets and past blocks of triple-deckers, corner stores, chain-link fences with plastic grocery bags caught in them like surrender flags. Nate had built Altieri in his head for years without ever admitting he was building him. The house in those private rende...
open_hooks_to_carry: Domestic rhythms: evenings, meals, the texture of cohabitation
Sarah referencing shared history Nate doesn't have — Providence trip, the fight about his mother, concert tickets
prev_tail_excerpt:
"You good to handle that?" he asked.

Nate looked at him. "Handle what."

"Eastbridge."

The word sat between them with all its regular office innocence. Ray only knew Dominic had been weird and sick and slower than usual. He didn't know what sat open on the desk.

"Yeah," Nate said.

Ray watched him for a moment.

"All right." He picked up the briefcase again. "Don't stay all night. If you melt into the carpet, Janice'll make me explain it Monday."

"I won't."

"And eat something."

That, apparently, was a company value.

Ray started away, got as far as the conference room, then came back. He shifted the briefcase strap against his palm and looked at Nate like he wanted to say one harder thing and had decided this was the version he could live with.

"For what it's worth," he said, "Vic likes you. More than most people around here. Don't make him regret it."

Then he was gone.

The office emptied by degrees after that. Accounting left first, then Janice at the desk downstairs, then somebody from drafting who whistled tunelessly all the way to the elevator before remembering the elevator was broken and swearing at the stairwell. Nate stayed where he was with the Eastbridge file open to Morton's name.

This was Dominic's desk. Dominic's phone. Dominic's handwriting on the tabs. The split in the blotter, the kinked phone cord, the bent paper clip by the keyboard: all of it had been here while Mort became Unit 7 and Linda became wife present. Nate had killed the man, taken his chair, and still had the toner on his thumb.
state_deltas_required: The intimacy of cohabitation — Sarah knows Dominic's body, habits, and emotional rhythms better than anyone, making her the most dangerous person in Nate's impersonation
Nate crosses from performing Dominic's relationship to inhabiting it. The betrayal deepens from practical fraud to moral violation. Sarah may prefer the imposter.
